ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for pork-lard-dressed rice. FOODON_00002678 (cooked rice food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E (Rice, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for lard-dressed rice dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for pork lard rice or lard-dressed rice dish.

preparation_method: seasoned with soy sauce and dark soy sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of pork lard rice is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed jasmine rice dressed with 20–25 g of rendered pork lard and crispy lard bits, plus soy sauce seasoning.

nutrient_source.title: Pork lard rice — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Lard (NDB 04002) and Cooked white rice, long-grain (NDB 20045), combined in representative proportions (approx. 67% cooked rice, 8% lard, 25% other components including soy sauce and garnish), cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for jasmine rice.
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for pork lard rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked long-grain white rice (NDB 20045) and lard (NDB 04002) in representative proportions typical of this dish (approximately 67 g cooked rice + 8 g lard per 100 g edible portion), with minor contributions from soy sauce sodium. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (jasmine-type, cooked) GI reference values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork lard rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: jasmine white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 68–89 (mean ~72) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The lard component does not contribute glycemic carbohydrates and does not materially alter the GI of the rice base. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 79.5) / 100 ≈ 57.2, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving ≈ 79.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g serving of pork lard rice contributes approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 37–42% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index white rice base with high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 72 and a glycemic load of approximately 57 per standard serving, pork lard rice causes a rapid postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, substitute with brown or parboiled rice where possible, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from rendered pork lard and elevated cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork lard rice provides approximately 3.2 g saturated fat and 18 mg cholesterol per 100 g; regular consumption may contribute to LDL elevation, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider leaner rice dish alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork lard rice (猪油拌饭, Zhu You Ban Fan; Thai: ข้าวน้ำมันหมู) is a simple but richly flavoured rice dish popular across Thai-Chinese communities in Thailand and among the Thai-Chinese diaspora in Singapore. It consists of freshly steamed jasmine rice — typically long-grain and fragrant — dressed generously with rendered pork lard (liquid fat) and topped with crispy pork lard bits (cracklings), then seasoned with light soy sauce and dark soy sauce, and garnished with sliced spring onion. The dish is prized for its glossy, unctuous texture and the savoury, umami-rich flavour imparted by the lard and soy sauce combination. The crispy lard bits add a contrasting crunch. Nutritionally, pork lard rice is a calorie-dense, high-fat, high-GI dish: per 100 g it provides approximately 195 kcal, 8.5 g fat (of which 3.2 g is saturated), and 26.5 g carbohydrate. The high glycemic index (~72) and large carbohydrate load per serving make it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The sodium contribution from soy sauce (~280 mg per 100 g) is relevant for hypertension management. The saturated fat content from pork lard warrants moderation for cardiovascular health. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants and hawker stalls, often as a budget comfort meal. It is not halal and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

jasmine rice: ข้าวหอมมะลิ

confidence_reason: No exact entry for pork lard rice exists in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked white rice and lard in representative proportions. GI is assigned by analogy to jasmine white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite dish estimated from component data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data (cooked long-grain white rice NDB 20045 and lard NDB 04002) in representative proportions (~67 g cooked rice + ~8 g lard per 100 g edible portion); no exact database entry exists for this dish. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish in the component sources used. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy lard entry and is not available for the composite estimate. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources for this dish. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 9. trans_fat_g is 0.0; naturally rendered pork lard contains negligible industrially produced trans fats (confirmed near-zero per USDA SR Legacy NDB 04002). 10. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on jasmine white rice per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for pork lard rice as a composite dish exists in approved sources. 11. The Malay local name 'Nasi Lemak Babi Goreng' is a descriptive approximation; there is no established standard Malay term for this dish. 12. This dish is not halal due to pork content. 13. The food_name has been normalised from 'Signature Pork Lard Rice' (menu-style) to 'Pork Lard Rice' (canonical English name).

improvement_actions_taken: Normalised food_name from menu-style 'Signature Pork Lard Rice' to canonical 'Pork Lard Rice'.
Added Thai local name in Thai script (ข้าวน้ำมันหมู) to satisfy G6 mandatory Thai language requirement.
Added Chinese simplified and traditional names (猪油拌饭 / 豬油拌飯).
Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names for Singapore diaspora context.
Estimated per_100g nutrients from USDA SR Legacy component data (NDB 20045 + NDB 04002).
Assigned GI of 72 by analogy to jasmine white rice per University of Sydney GI Database.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes field.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Added full health context for Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease.
Used closest_parent match_type for FoodOn and FoodEx2; none for SNOMED CT — no invented codes.
